one_line_takeaway: A model-less bag-of-words classifier beats or matches most LLM MIAs on their own post-hoc benchmarks, proving those benchmarks measure a temporal distribution shift rather than memorization; the paper hands the field a randomized replacement benchmark suite so future MIAs can be compared honestly.
key_finding: Across every available post-hoc dataset, a trivial word-count classifier that never queries the target model separates members from non-members far above chance -- often near-perfectly and above the MIA AUCs those datasets were used to claim: WikiMIA 0.988-0.994, BookMIA 0.937, Gutenberg (full) 0.997. This means high reported MIA AUCs cannot be attributed to LLM memorization; they largely reflect a temporal language/formatting distribution shift (e.g. year tokens in WikiMIA, Project Gutenberg boilerplate templates, 'didn't' vs 'did not' in BookMIA). Conversely, on properly randomized splits (Pile/MIMIR 13_0.8), both the BoW baseline (~0.50) AND all evaluated MIAs collapse to near-random (Table IV, all ~0.47-0.54 on Pythia-6.9B) -- newer attacks do NOT beat older ones or a coin flip. Signal only re-emerges in controlled canary injection (Ratio-LLaMA-2 reaches AUC 0.704 at 1000 repetitions) and in the RDD setup the BoW shift drops from 0.69 to 0.534. Over-aggressive post-hoc deduplication (MIMIR 7_0.2) itself REINTRODUCES a shift (e.g. Github BoW 0.860), so deduplication is not a free fix.
core_contribution: A Systematization-of-Knowledge (IEEE SaTML 2025) that argues the recent wave of Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) against LLMs is evaluated on fundamentally broken benchmarks. The paper's OWN contributions are: (1) a structured literature review (Table I) of 20+ MIA studies distinguishing target (pretraining vs finetuning), unit of interest (sequence/document/dataset), motivation (privacy/copyright/data-contamination), and dataset, showing that >=10 new MIAs proposed in '24 are almost all evaluated on data collected 'post-hoc' (members = data from known training sources, non-members = same sources after the model's cutoff date); (2) a model-less bag-of-words (BoW) classifier used as a diagnostic to quantify the members/non-members distribution shift WITHOUT touching the target model, demonstrating across 6 post-hoc datasets that this shift alone explains (and often exceeds) the reported MIA performance; and (3) a critical evaluation of four ways forward (randomized held-out splits, injected unique canary/trap sequences, finetuning-based setups, and post-hoc control methods including a newly-proposed Regression Discontinuity Design adaptation), culminating in a concrete recommended benchmark suite (Table VI). Note: the distribution-shift CONCERN was first raised by cited work (Duan et al. [27], Maini et al. [65]); the paper's added value is the systematic BoW quantification across many datasets, the re-benchmarking of MIAs on properly randomized setups, and the solution catalogue.
threat_model: The papers it systematizes assume an attacker A with access to a released target LLM M (trained on dataset D) who wants to infer whether a specific piece of text (sequence, document, or whole dataset) was in D. Access assumptions range from white-box (weights), to black-box (query logits), to no-box (generated text only), with some attacks also assuming access to non-member data from the target distribution. This SoK itself is method-agnostic to the threat model: it targets the EVALUATION methodology (how members/non-members are constructed), not any single attack. The security stakes are real: broken MIA evaluation leads to false conclusions about LLM privacy leakage, copyright-infringement detection, benchmark contamination, and the effectiveness of privacy defenses (watermarking, machine unlearning).
limitations: The authors are explicit that the BoW AUC is only a LOWER BOUND on distribution shift -- a near-random BoW score does not prove IID members/non-members, since subtler shifts could be caught by stronger classifiers. Each proposed remedy has caveats they state directly: randomized Pile splits still suffer heavy member/non-member overlap (Duan reports 32.5% average 7-gram overlap for Wikipedia, up to 76.9% for Github), making membership ill-defined; canary/trap injection measures a worst-case, possibly unrealistic risk tied to sequence length and perplexity and can favor certain attacks (e.g. reference-model-based Ratio); finetuning-based evaluation may not translate to full-scale pretraining; and RDD assumes a known cutoff date, needs frequently-updated sources (works for ArXiv, not crime novels), and does not resolve overlap. Recommended splits (Pythia/GPT-NEO/CroissantLLM/OpenLLaMA) exclude the most-used production models (GPT-4, LLaMA-2/3, Mistral), for which no clean randomized setup exists. The work is concurrent with Das et al. [21], which independently proposed a similar 'blind' BoW baseline on a subset of these datasets.

datasets_used: WikiMIA (32- and 128-token excerpts, Shi et al.) -- post-hoc
BookMIA (copyright-protected book excerpts, Shi et al.) -- post-hoc
Gutenberg full and mid (Project Gutenberg books, Meeus et al.) -- post-hoc
ArXivMIA (paper abstracts, Liu et al.) -- post-hoc
StackMIAsub (Stack Exchange, Ye et al.) -- post-hoc
MIMIR (Duan et al., deduplicated Pile: 13_0.8 and 7_0.2 variants)
Copyright-trap canaries injected in CroissantLLM (Meeus et al.)
OpenLLaMA ArXiv RDD set (papers Feb '23 members vs Mar '23 non-members)
quantitative_results: BoW AUC on post-hoc data (Table II): WikiMIA(32)=0.988, WikiMIA(128)=0.994, BookMIA=0.937, Gutenberg(full)=0.997, Gutenberg(mid)=0.889, ArXiv=0.690, ArXivMIA=0.596, StackMIAsub=0.612
Reference MIA AUCs those datasets claimed: Shi et al. WikiMIA (LLaMA-30B)=0.74, BookMIA (GPT-3)=0.88; Meeus et al. books=0.86, papers=0.68 -- BoW meets or exceeds these
BoW on randomized Pile splits (Table III): ~0.48-0.55 across all 7 subsets (near random)
BoW on MIMIR 13_0.8: near random except Github=0.679; MIMIR 7_0.2 (over-dedup) reintroduces shift: PubMed=0.783, ArXiv=0.730, DM Math=0.799, Github=0.860
MIAs on MIMIR/Pythia-dedup-6.9B (Table IV, 500+500 samples): all attacks near-random, ~0.47-0.54; e.g. Min-K% Prob 0.515 (Wikipedia), Ratio-LLaMA-2 0.537 (ArXiv)
Canary traps in CroissantLLM (Table V, 500+500): at 1000 repetitions best = Ratio-LLaMA-2 AUC=0.704, Min-K% Prob=0.626, Lower=0.613; BoW stays 0.543
RDD on OpenLLaMA: BoW distribution-shift AUC drops to 0.534 (vs 0.69 for the same papers used post-hoc by Meeus, and 0.60 for ArXivMIA abstracts); MIAs stay near random
Member/non-member overlap in Pile (from Duan et al.): 32.5% average 7-gram overlap for Wikipedia, up to 76.9% for Github
baselines_compared: Bag-of-words random forest (the paper's model-less baseline / prior)
Min-K% Prob (Shi et al.) and Min-K%++ (Zhang et al.)
PAC (Ye et al.), SURP (Zhang et al.)
ReCaLL (Xie et al.), CON-ReCall (Wang et al.), Probe (Liu et al.)
